label: Weight Decay
definition: A regularizer that continually pulls the weights toward zero, either by adding a squared-magnitude penalty to the loss or by shrinking the weights directly, which favors simpler, better-generalizing solutions. Its non-obvious twist is that those two views, identical for plain SGD, come apart for Adam, which is why AdamW exists and now trains most large models.

body.text: Weight decay biases training toward small parameter values. In its classic form it adds a term to the loss function proportional to the sum of the squared weights, the L2 penalty, so the optimizer must now balance fitting the data against keeping the weights small, and at every gradient descent step the extra term contributes a force pulling each weight a little toward zero. The name comes from that continual shrinkage: absent a gradient pushing back, a weight steadily decays.

It endures because it is one of the most reliable defenses against overfitting. A model with large, finely tuned weights can carve a complicated decision surface that memorizes the training data, noise included, and then fails on new examples; penalizing weight magnitude expresses a preference for smoother, simpler functions that usually generalize better. In the loss landscape it nudges the optimizer toward wider, flatter local attractor basins rather than sharp ones, and those flatter solutions are the ones that transfer to unseen data. A single coefficient sets the strength, often the most impactful regularization knob to tune.

For plain gradient descent the two ways of seeing it are nearly the same. Adding the squared-weight penalty to the loss makes its gradient include a term proportional to the weights themselves, so the update subtracts a fraction of each weight on top of following the data gradient, which is identical to multiplying every weight by a number slightly less than one each step, hence decay. The coefficient controls how aggressive the shrinkage is: too little and regularization does nothing, too much and the model is pulled so hard toward zero that it underfits.

The equivalence breaks with adaptive optimizers, and the subtlety is worth knowing because it changed common practice. In Adam, folding the L2 penalty into the gradient causes it to be rescaled by the same per-parameter second-moment denominator that adapts the step size, so weights with large gradients receive less effective decay and the regularization is distorted. AdamW repairs this by decoupling weight decay from the adaptive update, shrinking the weights by a fixed fraction outside the gradient computation entirely. The decoupled form generalizes better and is now standard for training large models, which is why "L2 regularization" and "true weight decay," casually treated as synonyms, are not actually the same thing.

image.alt: Plot of ridge-regression coefficient values against the regularization strength alpha on a log scale; as alpha grows the many coefficient curves all shrink and converge toward zero.
image.caption: Weight decay adds an L2 penalty on the size of the weights, pulling them toward zero. As the regularization strength rises (moving toward larger alpha), every coefficient shrinks toward zero, trading a little fit for the smaller, simpler weights that tend to generalize better.
